icd_category: K27.1 — Peptic ulcer, acute with perforation
one_liner: A 58-year-old male presents with sudden-onset severe epigastric pain, board-like abdominal rigidity, and tachycardia after years of NSAID use.

stage2.hpi_extension: Patient reports the sudden onset of severe, 10/10 epigastric pain approximately 4 hours ago while at work. The pain began abruptly ('like a knife'), initially localized to the epigastrium but has since become diffuse across the entire abdomen. He is lying completely still and reports that any movement worsens the pain. He had one episode of dry heaving but no frank vomiting. He endorses a long history of intermittent epigastric burning and discomfort over the past 2 years, often worse on an empty stomach and partially relieved by antacids. He has been taking ibuprofen 800 mg three times daily for the past 4 months for chronic knee pain. He skipped lunch today. He denies hematemesis, melena, hematochezia, fever, recent travel, or prior abdominal surgeries.
stage2.pmh: Osteoarthritis of both knees, hypertension, history of H. pylori infection (treated 5 years ago, compliance uncertain)
stage2.medications: Ibuprofen 800 mg TID, lisinopril 20 mg daily, occasional over-the-counter antacids
stage2.family_history: Father had gastric ulcer. No family history of gastric cancer.
stage2.social_history: Smokes 1 pack per day for 30 years. Drinks 2-3 beers daily. Works in construction. Divorced, lives alone.
stage2.physical_exam: General: Thin male in severe distress, lying motionless, diaphoretic, and pale. HEENT: Dry mucous membranes. Neck: Supple. Cardiovascular: Tachycardic, regular rhythm; No murmurs. Pulmonary: Shallow respirations; Decreased breath sounds at the right base. Abdomen: Board-like rigidity; Diffuse tenderness most pronounced in the epigastrium; Rebound tenderness present in all quadrants; Involuntary guarding; Absent bowel sounds; No distension; No hernia. Extremities: Cool, delayed capillary refill (>3 seconds); No edema. Skin: Diaphoretic, pale.

lab_results.clinical_significance: Elevated, consistent with dehydration and prerenal state
Mildly elevated, likely prerenal from volume depletion
Normal, helps exclude pancreatitis as the primary etiology
Elevated, reflecting tissue hypoperfusion and early sepsis physiology
Mild acidosis, consistent with hypoperfusion and lactic acidosis

imaging_results.result: Free air (pneumoperitoneum) visible under the right hemidiaphragm. No pleural effusion. No pneumothorax.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Pneumoperitoneum is pathognomonic for a perforated hollow viscus. In the setting of epigastric pain and NSAID use, this condition is the leading diagnosis.

imaging_results.result: Large amount of free intraperitoneal air. Free fluid in the upper abdomen and pelvis. Extraluminal contrast seen near the anterior first portion of the duodenum, consistent with a perforated duodenal ulcer. No abscess. No bowel obstruction.
imaging_results.clinical_significance: Confirms perforated duodenal ulcer with localization of the perforation site. Guides surgical planning.

low_yield_tests: Upper GI series with water-soluble contrast (delays treatment)
H. pylori serology (does not change acute management)
stage3.final_clue: Upright chest X-ray reveals pneumoperitoneum under the right hemidiaphragm. CT abdomen confirms free air and extraluminal contrast near the anterior duodenum, diagnostic of a perforated duodenal ulcer. Combined with the acute peritonitis on exam, NSAID use, and smoking history, this patient requires emergent surgical repair.

stage5.final_clue: Upright chest X-ray reveals pneumoperitoneum under the right hemidiaphragm. CT abdomen shows free air and extraluminal contrast near the anterior duodenum.

debrief.classic_presentation: Sudden-onset, severe ('thunderclap') epigastric pain with rapid progression to diffuse peritonitis. Board-like abdominal rigidity is classic. Risk factors include chronic NSAID use, smoking, H. pylori infection, and physiologic stress. Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate into the peritoneal cavity (causing peritonitis), while posterior ulcers erode into the gastroduodenal artery (causing hemorrhage).
debrief.pathophysiology: Peptic ulcers result from an imbalance between mucosal defense mechanisms (mucus, bicarbonate, prostaglandins) and aggressive factors (acid, pepsin, H. pylori, NSAIDs). NSAIDs inhibit COX-1, reducing prostaglandin synthesis and impairing mucosal blood flow and bicarbonate secretion. Full-thickness ulceration leads to perforation, with spillage of gastroduodenal contents (acid, bile, bacteria) into the peritoneal cavity, causing chemical and then bacterial peritonitis. Untreated perforation leads to sepsis, multi-organ failure, and death.
debrief.treatment_overview: Emergent surgical repair is the standard of care. Options include primary repair with omental (Graham) patch via laparoscopic or open approach. Aggressive IV fluid resuscitation with crystalloids. Broad-spectrum antibiotics (piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone plus metronidazole). NGT decompression. NPO. Discontinue NSAIDs permanently. Post-operative PPI therapy (IV pantoprazole then oral). Test and treat H. pylori. In select patients with small, contained perforations and minimal contamination, non-operative (Taylor) management with NGT, IV antibiotics, and PPI may be considered, though this is controversial.
high_yield_pearls: Pneumoperitoneum on upright CXR is present in approximately 80% of perforated peptic ulcers; its absence does not exclude perforation.
Anterior duodenal ulcers perforate (peritonitis), posterior duodenal ulcers bleed (gastroduodenal artery erosion).
NSAIDs and H. pylori are the two most common causes of peptic ulcer disease, and their effects are synergistic.
Graham patch (omental patch) repair is the classic surgical technique for perforated duodenal ulcer.
Free air under the diaphragm is best detected on upright CXR or left lateral decubitus abdominal film; CT is the most sensitive study overall.
sources: Schwartz's Principles of Surgery, 11th Edition, Ch. 26 — https://accessmedicine.mhmedical.com/book.aspx?bookID=2576
Lau JY et al. Lancet 2017;390:613-624 (Peptic Ulcer Disease) — https://www.thelancet.com/action/doSearch?text1=Lau+JY+et+al.+Lancet+2017%3B390%3A613-624+%28Peptic+Ulcer+Disease%29
Søreide K et al. World J Emerg Surg 2015;10:34 (Perforated Peptic Ulcer) — https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/?term=S%C3%B8reide+K+et+al.+World+J+Emerg+Surg+2015%3B10%3A34+%28Perforated+Peptic+Ulcer%29
